key_insight: Optimized resistance spot welding can make dissimilar aluminum–steel joints achieve fatigue strength comparable to same-material joints, despite intermetallic compound formation at the interface.
engineering_relevance: For automotive body structures, this suggests multi-material joints can be designed for durability if interfacial reactions and weld geometry are controlled.
method: Resistance spot welded steel sheets and aluminum alloy sheets under optimized welding conditions and electrode selection.
Examined fatigue crack initiation and propagation behavior in the dissimilar welds.
Related fatigue fracture behavior to intermetallic compound layer thickness/distribution and aluminum-side indentation shape.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that fatigue strength of the aluminum–steel joints was similar to same-material aluminum and steel joints, with crack initiation and unstable propagation observed in the intermetallic layer.
watch_item: Fatigue performance depends strongly on intermetallic-layer morphology and indentation geometry, so process control is critical and results may not transfer to poorly optimized welds.
paper_title: Elucidation of fatigue crack propagation behavior in dissimilar aluminum alloy and steel joints from resistance spot welding
